title: Risk analysis and information system security policies
description: Establish and maintain policies on risk analysis and information system security covering all space and ground segment assets.
evidenceGuidance: Documented risk assessment methodology, asset inventory with classification, risk register with treatment plans, approved security policies, annual review records.
applicabilityNotes: For space operators: must cover satellite bus systems, ground station networks, TT&C links, and mission control infrastructure. Consider RF-specific risks (jamming, spoofing) alongside traditional IT risks.
plainLanguageExplanation: You need a written-down process for figuring out what could go wrong with your satellite systems and how bad it would be. This means looking at everything from your ground stations to your space links and deciding which risks to fix, which to accept, and which to monitor. Review it at least once a year.
evidenceExamples: Risk assessment document covering space and ground segments, reviewed within 12 months
Asset register in SpaceGuard with criticality ratings for all assets
Risk treatment plan showing decisions for each identified risk
Board-approved information security policy with space-specific sections
commonMistakes: Copying a generic IT risk framework without adapting it to satellite operations
Not including RF link risks (jamming, spoofing, replay) in the risk register
Treating risk assessment as a one-time exercise instead of continuous

title: Space asset classification and criticality assessment
description: Classify all space assets by criticality to service delivery and assess their exposure to cyber threats across the full mission lifecycle.
evidenceGuidance: Asset register with criticality ratings, dependency mapping between space and ground segments, threat exposure assessment per asset class.
applicabilityNotes: Map dependencies between orbital assets and ground infrastructure. A compromised ground station can affect all satellites it controls.
plainLanguageExplanation: Every piece of your space infrastructure needs a criticality label. Your LEO constellation is probably CRITICAL because losing it means losing revenue. Your test lab might be LOW. The point is to know what matters most so you protect it first. Map which ground systems control which satellites.
evidenceExamples: Complete asset inventory in SpaceGuard with ENISA segment classification
Dependency map showing which ground stations control which satellites
Criticality assessment for each asset based on service impact analysis
Annual review record of asset classifications
commonMistakes: Classifying everything as CRITICAL, which means nothing gets prioritized
Forgetting inter-satellite links and data relay paths in the dependency map
Not updating classifications when mission parameters change

title: Incident handling procedures
description: Implement procedures for detecting, managing, and resolving cybersecurity incidents affecting space infrastructure.
evidenceGuidance: Incident response plan, defined severity levels, escalation procedures, roles and responsibilities matrix, incident log with timestamps.
applicabilityNotes: Space-specific incidents include telemetry anomalies, unauthorized telecommands, RF interference events, and ground segment intrusions.
plainLanguageExplanation: If something goes wrong with your satellite or ground station because of a cyber attack, you need a documented plan for how your team responds. This is not optional. NIS2 requires you to have this written down and tested, with clear roles for who does what during an incident.
evidenceExamples: Incident Response Plan, version-controlled, reviewed within 12 months
Record of at least one IR drill conducted in the past year
Severity classification matrix covering space-specific scenarios
On-call rotation schedule with contact details for all roles
commonMistakes: Generic IT incident plan that does not cover satellite-specific scenarios like telemetry anomalies
Not testing the plan with a drill. A document that has never been exercised is not evidence of readiness.
Forgetting to include ground station service providers in the communication chain

title: Incident notification to authorities
description: Notify the competent authority or CSIRT of significant incidents: early warning within 24 hours, incident notification within 72 hours, final report within one month.
evidenceGuidance: Notification templates, submission records with timestamps, evidence of meeting 24h/72h/1month deadlines, designated point of contact.
applicabilityNotes: Under the proposed EU Space Act, operators may also need to report to EUSPA within 12 hours. Prepare for dual reporting obligations.
plainLanguageExplanation: When a significant cyber incident hits your space operations, you must tell the authorities quickly: a heads-up within 24 hours, a proper notification within 72 hours, and a full report within a month. The EU Space Act may add a 12-hour EUSPA deadline on top of this. Have templates ready so you are not writing reports from scratch during a crisis.
evidenceExamples: Pre-filled notification templates for 24h, 72h, and final reports
Log of all past notifications with submission timestamps proving deadline compliance
Designated NIS2 point of contact registered with national CSIRT
Procedure document describing how to classify an incident as 'significant'
commonMistakes: Not having templates ready, leading to missed 24-hour deadlines during real incidents
Unclear criteria for what counts as 'significant', causing either over-reporting or under-reporting
Assuming your managed service provider handles notification. The obligation is on the operator.

title: Incident detection and monitoring capabilities
description: Deploy technical capabilities to detect cybersecurity incidents in real-time across space and ground infrastructure.
evidenceGuidance: Security monitoring architecture, log sources and retention policy, alerting rules documentation, evidence of monitoring coverage.
applicabilityNotes: Must monitor both IT systems (mission control, data processing) and OT/space-specific systems (TT&C interfaces, telemetry streams, RF link quality).
plainLanguageExplanation: You need actual technical systems watching your infrastructure around the clock, not just a policy saying you will. This means collecting logs from your ground stations, monitoring telemetry for anomalies, watching your RF links for interference, and having alerting rules that wake someone up when something looks wrong.
evidenceExamples: SpaceGuard detection rules configured for your asset types and telemetry streams
Log retention policy showing at least 6 months of security-relevant logs
Alert coverage map showing which assets have active monitoring
Evidence of 24/7 monitoring capability (SOC contract or on-call rotation)
commonMistakes: Monitoring IT systems but not TT&C interfaces or RF link quality
Collecting logs but never reviewing them or setting up alerts
No correlation between telemetry anomalies and security events

title: Business continuity and disaster recovery
description: Maintain business continuity plans including backup management, disaster recovery, and crisis management for space operations.
evidenceGuidance: Business continuity plan, disaster recovery procedures, backup schedules and test results, crisis management team roster, annual BCP test records.
applicabilityNotes: Consider satellite redundancy, backup ground station procedures, failover for mission-critical data links, and recovery procedures for compromised spacecraft.
plainLanguageExplanation: What happens if your primary ground station goes down? Or if ransomware hits your mission control center? You need a plan that covers how to keep your satellites safe and your service running. This includes backup ground stations, backup communication paths, and tested recovery procedures.
evidenceExamples: Business Continuity Plan with space operations annex, tested within 12 months
Backup ground station agreements with documented failover procedures
DR test report showing successful failover of mission-critical systems
Crisis management team roster with alternates and contact details
commonMistakes: BCP that covers IT systems but ignores satellite safe-mode procedures
Never testing the backup ground station failover procedure
Assuming cloud hosting equals disaster recovery without verifying RPO/RTO

title: Mission continuity under cyber attack
description: Ensure continued mission operations during and after a cybersecurity incident, including safe-mode procedures for affected spacecraft.
evidenceGuidance: Mission degradation procedures, satellite safe-mode activation protocols, backup communication paths, service continuity agreements.
applicabilityNotes: Define procedures for maintaining satellite health when ground control is compromised. Include autonomous satellite operations capability assessment.
plainLanguageExplanation: If your ground systems get hacked, your satellites are still up there. You need procedures for putting them in a safe state autonomously, switching to backup control paths, and gracefully degrading your service rather than losing it entirely. This is unique to space and a key differentiator from standard IT continuity.
evidenceExamples: Satellite safe-mode activation procedure triggered by loss of authenticated ground contact
Documented backup command uplink path through an alternate ground station
Service degradation plan showing which capabilities are preserved at each level
Autonomous operations assessment for each satellite type
commonMistakes: No procedure for satellite safe-mode when ground control is compromised
Assuming the satellite will just keep working without ground contact
Not having a pre-authenticated backup command path through an independent ground station

title: Supply chain security
description: Assess and manage cybersecurity risks in the supply chain, including relationships with direct suppliers and service providers.
evidenceGuidance: Supplier security assessment records, contractual security requirements, vendor risk register, third-party audit results or certifications.
applicabilityNotes: Assess COTS component suppliers, ground station service providers, launch service cybersecurity, and cloud providers hosting mission data.
plainLanguageExplanation: Your satellite is only as secure as the weakest link in your supply chain. That cheap COTS component might have a backdoor. Your ground station provider might have poor access controls. NIS2 requires you to assess and manage these risks, not just trust your suppliers. Track who provides what and verify their security posture.
evidenceExamples: Supplier risk register in SpaceGuard covering all direct suppliers
Security requirements in supplier contracts (specific clauses, not just 'best practices')
Annual supplier security assessment results or valid certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2)
SBOM for all COTS software components with known vulnerability tracking
commonMistakes: Assuming ISO 27001 certification means the supplier is secure for space operations
Not assessing launch service providers for cybersecurity risk
Having security requirements in contracts but never verifying compliance

title: Ground segment provider security assessment
description: Evaluate the cybersecurity posture of all ground station operators, data relay providers, and ground-segment-as-a-service vendors.
evidenceGuidance: Vendor security questionnaire results, SLAs with security clauses, evidence of periodic reassessment, access control reviews.
applicabilityNotes: Many operators use shared ground station networks. Each provider must be assessed for their ability to protect command uplinks and telemetry downlinks.
plainLanguageExplanation: If you use KSAT, SSC, or any other ground station network, you are trusting them with your satellite command link. A compromised ground station could send unauthorized commands to your spacecraft. You must evaluate their security, put requirements in your contracts, and check again periodically.
evidenceExamples: Completed security questionnaire for each ground station provider
SLA with specific cybersecurity clauses (access control, monitoring, incident notification)
Evidence of annual reassessment of ground station provider security
Access control review showing who at each provider can access your satellite links
commonMistakes: Using a shared ground station network without assessing the provider's security
Trusting the provider's marketing claims instead of conducting an actual assessment
Not including incident notification requirements in the ground station SLA

title: Security in system acquisition, development, and maintenance
description: Embed security throughout the lifecycle of network and information systems, including vulnerability handling and disclosure.
evidenceGuidance: Secure development lifecycle documentation, code review records, vulnerability scanning reports, patch management policy, responsible disclosure policy.
applicabilityNotes: Includes flight software development, ground segment software, firmware updates to spacecraft (where possible), and mission planning tools.
plainLanguageExplanation: Security cannot be bolted on after your satellite is in orbit. It needs to be part of the design from Phase 0. This means secure coding practices for flight software, security testing before deployment, and a process for handling vulnerabilities discovered after launch. CRA adds specific SBOM requirements on top.
evidenceExamples: Secure development lifecycle policy covering flight and ground software
Code review records for recent flight software updates
Vulnerability scanning reports for ground segment systems
Responsible disclosure policy published and accessible to security researchers
commonMistakes: Treating flight software as immune to vulnerabilities because it is in orbit
No process for handling vulnerability reports from external researchers
Security testing only at the end of development instead of throughout the lifecycle

title: Vulnerability management for space systems
description: Maintain a process for identifying, assessing, and remediating vulnerabilities in space and ground segment systems.
evidenceGuidance: Vulnerability scanning schedule and results, patch deployment records, risk acceptance documentation for unpatched systems, CVE tracking.
applicabilityNotes: Many satellite systems cannot be patched in orbit. Document risk acceptance for known vulnerabilities and compensating controls at the ground segment.
plainLanguageExplanation: You need to actively look for vulnerabilities in your systems, not wait for them to be exploited. For ground systems, this means regular scanning and patching. For flight software, it means tracking known CVEs in your SBOM and documenting compensating controls when you cannot patch. Risk acceptance must be a deliberate decision, not an oversight.
evidenceExamples: SBOM tracked in SpaceGuard with automated CVE matching for all components
Quarterly vulnerability scan results for ground segment systems
Patch deployment records with timeline from disclosure to remediation
Documented risk acceptance with compensating controls for unpatched flight software
commonMistakes: No SBOM for flight software, making it impossible to track component vulnerabilities
Accepting risk on known vulnerabilities without documenting compensating controls
Patching ground systems but ignoring vulnerabilities in COTS spacecraft components

title: Effectiveness assessment of cybersecurity measures
description: Implement policies and procedures to assess whether cybersecurity risk-management measures are effective.
evidenceGuidance: Penetration test reports, security audit results, compliance assessment records, KPI tracking, management review minutes.
applicabilityNotes: Under the proposed EU Space Act, operators must conduct Threat-Led Penetration Testing (TLPT) prior to launch and every three years.
plainLanguageExplanation: Having security controls is not enough. You need to prove they actually work. This means penetration testing your ground segment, auditing your processes, and tracking metrics over time. The EU Space Act will require TLPT before launch and every three years, so start planning now.
evidenceExamples: Annual penetration test report covering ground segment and command interfaces
TLPT report (or schedule showing next TLPT date)
Security KPI dashboard showing trends (MTTD, MTTR, compliance score)
Management review minutes documenting security effectiveness discussion
commonMistakes: Penetration testing the web application but not the TT&C interfaces
Not tracking security metrics over time to show improvement
Treating the annual audit as a checkbox exercise instead of a genuine assessment

title: Cyber hygiene and training
description: Ensure basic cyber hygiene practices and provide cybersecurity training to all personnel.
evidenceGuidance: Training program documentation, attendance records, phishing simulation results, security awareness materials, role-based training records.
applicabilityNotes: Training should cover: recognizing anomalous telemetry, handling satellite command credentials, RF interference awareness, and social engineering defense.
plainLanguageExplanation: Everyone on your team needs to understand the basics of cybersecurity, but satellite operators need more than standard corporate training. Your engineers need to recognize when telemetry looks suspicious. Your operators need to understand why they should not share command credentials. Training must be role-specific and tested, not just a video everyone clicks through.
evidenceExamples: Annual training program with role-specific modules for operators, engineers, and managers
Attendance records showing 100% completion for personnel with system access
Phishing simulation results showing improvement over time
Space-specific training module covering telemetry anomaly recognition and credential handling
commonMistakes: Using generic corporate cybersecurity training without space-specific content
Not requiring training for contractors and ground station operators
No measurement of training effectiveness beyond attendance tracking

title: Cryptography and encryption policies
description: Establish policies on the use of cryptography and encryption for protecting data in transit and at rest.
evidenceGuidance: Encryption policy, key management procedures, inventory of cryptographic implementations, crypto-agility assessment, certificate management.
applicabilityNotes: Space-specific: TT&C link encryption (SDLS/OTAR), telemetry encryption, ground network encryption. Assess post-quantum cryptography readiness.
plainLanguageExplanation: You need a clear policy on what gets encrypted, with what algorithms, and how keys are managed. For satellite operators, this includes your TT&C links, telemetry data, and ground network traffic. Start thinking about post-quantum cryptography now because your satellites may still be in orbit when current algorithms are broken.
evidenceExamples: Cryptography policy covering space link, ground network, and data-at-rest encryption
Inventory of all cryptographic implementations with algorithm and key length
Key management procedure covering generation, distribution, rotation, and destruction
Post-quantum cryptography readiness assessment
commonMistakes: Encrypting ground network traffic but leaving TT&C links unencrypted
No crypto-agility plan for transitioning to post-quantum algorithms
Key management procedures that do not cover emergency key revocation

title: Space link encryption and key management
description: Implement and maintain encryption for satellite command and telemetry links with appropriate key management procedures.
evidenceGuidance: Link encryption specifications, key generation and distribution procedures, key rotation schedule, emergency key revocation procedures.
applicabilityNotes: SDLS compliance where applicable. Consider key rotation challenges for satellites with limited on-board crypto capability.
plainLanguageExplanation: Your satellite command link is the most critical communication path you have. If someone can inject commands, they control your spacecraft. Encrypting this link with proper key management is fundamental. CCSDS SDLS is the standard. Key rotation is harder in space than on the ground, so plan for it during design.
evidenceExamples: SDLS implementation certificate or compliance documentation
Key generation and pre-loading procedure for spacecraft crypto units
Key rotation schedule (OTAR if supported, or per-mission key loading)
Emergency key revocation procedure with tested execution timeline
commonMistakes: Relying on security through obscurity instead of proper link encryption
No key rotation plan for long-duration missions
Emergency key revocation procedure that has never been tested

title: Human resources security and access control
description: Implement human resources security measures, access control policies, and asset management procedures.
evidenceGuidance: Access control policy, user access reviews, privileged access management records, background check procedures, offboarding checklists.
applicabilityNotes: Privileged access to satellite command systems must be tightly controlled. Implement separation of duties for critical commands.
plainLanguageExplanation: Control who can access what, especially satellite command systems. Not everyone needs the ability to send commands to your spacecraft. Use role-based access, review permissions regularly, and make sure people lose access immediately when they leave. For critical commands, require two people (dual authorization).
evidenceExamples: Access control policy with role definitions for satellite command, telemetry, and admin
Quarterly user access review records for mission-critical systems
Privileged access management system for satellite command authorization
Offboarding checklist showing credential revocation within 24 hours of departure
commonMistakes: Shared accounts for satellite command systems making individual accountability impossible
No separation of duties for critical spacecraft commands
Slow offboarding process where departed personnel retain access for weeks

title: Multi-factor authentication
description: Deploy multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication for access to critical systems.
evidenceGuidance: MFA implementation records, coverage assessment, exception documentation, continuous authentication evaluation.
applicabilityNotes: MFA must cover: mission control access, ground station remote access, satellite command interfaces, and cloud-hosted mission data platforms.
plainLanguageExplanation: Passwords alone are not enough for systems that control satellites. Every access point to your mission-critical systems needs a second factor: hardware tokens, authenticator apps, or certificates. No exceptions for convenience. If someone compromises a password, MFA is what stops them from commanding your spacecraft.
evidenceExamples: MFA coverage report showing all mission-critical systems protected
Hardware token inventory for satellite command operators
Exception register for any systems where MFA is not yet implemented, with remediation timeline
Continuous authentication evaluation for high-security operator workstations
commonMistakes: MFA on the corporate VPN but not on the satellite command interface itself
SMS-based MFA which is vulnerable to SIM swapping attacks
Exception list that grows over time without remediation deadlines

title: Secured emergency communication systems
description: Maintain secured voice, video, text, and emergency communication systems within the entity.
evidenceGuidance: Emergency communication plan, secure communication tools inventory, out-of-band communication procedures, annual test records.
applicabilityNotes: Space operators need out-of-band communication capability in case primary ground networks are compromised.
plainLanguageExplanation: When your primary systems are compromised, you still need to communicate securely with your team, your ground station providers, and the authorities. This means having a backup communication channel that does not depend on your normal infrastructure. Test it regularly because you will not want to figure it out during an actual crisis.
evidenceExamples: Emergency communication plan with out-of-band channels (satellite phone, encrypted messaging)
Inventory of secure communication tools issued to key personnel
Annual test record of emergency communication system
Contact tree for crisis scenarios with verified phone numbers and encrypted messaging handles
commonMistakes: Emergency communication plan that relies on the same network that might be compromised
Key personnel do not know how to use the emergency communication tools
Emergency contact information that is out of date
